text: The surgeon disinfects and cleans the perianal area using soap and water, preparing the site for the procedure.

The surgeon exposes the fistula with a posterior internal opening and a high transphincteric tract. Curettage is performed to remove granulomas and clean the fistula tract, visibly clearing the tissue within the tract.

The surgeon uses endoscopic brushes to clean the narrow fistula tract, although a metallic curette is typically used. This step is crucial for activating and creating a suitable microenvironment for cellular nesting.
The surgeon leaves the seton in place during the procedure, ensuring visibility of the internal opening of the fistula.

The surgeon closes the internal opening of the fistula using a circular needle with an absorbable synthetic monofilament suture. The sutures are placed thickly to ensure a hermetic seal, and the satin is removed just before tying the knots. Three interrupted sutures are made to secure the closure.

The surgeon inverts the vial containing thirty million cells and carefully extracts the product using an eighteen gauge needle, transferring it into four screw-type syringes of ten milliliters each. The extraction is performed slowly to prevent cell lysis caused by strong suction.

The surgeon begins slow injections at the internal opening using a long, 21-gauge green needle. Multiple injection points are utilized to prevent product loss due to pressure. Half of the dose is injected into the internal opening, while the other half is administered from the external opening towards the internal opening into the adjacent tissue. The injection is made approximately 2-3 millimeters deep into the walls of the fistula.
The surgeon gently massages the area where the injection was administered, and then covers the external opening with a small adhesive bandage.
